passage.title: Lecture: The Carbon Footprint of Food
passage.body: Professor: Today I want to talk about something that affects every single one of us multiple times a day — what we eat, and what that means for the planet. When we measure the carbon footprint of food, we're looking at all the greenhouse gases produced across the entire supply chain: from land use and farming, to processing, transport, and even what happens to leftovers. And the differences between food types are striking. Beef, for instance, produces on average around 60 kilograms of CO2 equivalent per kilogram of food. Lamb is similarly high. Pork and chicken are considerably lower — around 7 and 6 kilograms respectively. But plant-based foods really stand out. Tofu comes in at roughly 3 kilograms, lentils at under 1. The reason meat is so carbon-intensive is largely down to methane from livestock digestion, land clearing for grazing, and the enormous quantities of feed crops required. Shifting even partially toward plant-based eating can meaningfully reduce an individual's total carbon footprint.

passage.title: Campus Conversation: Managing Academic Stress
passage.body:
Student: I've been meaning to come in for a while. I'm really struggling with stress this semester and I don't quite know how to handle it.
Advisor: You're definitely not alone — this is one of the most common things students come to talk to me about. Can you tell me a bit more about what's going on?
Student: It's mainly the workload. I feel like no matter how much I study, I'm always behind. I'm sleeping badly and I've stopped doing things I enjoy.
Advisor: That pattern — giving up hobbies and sacrificing sleep — actually makes stress worse in the long run. Research consistently shows that regular breaks and physical activity improve concentration and academic performance.
Student: So I shouldn't feel guilty about taking time off?
Advisor: Exactly. Building in recovery time isn't a luxury — it's a study strategy. I'd also suggest trying to break tasks into smaller chunks rather than looking at your whole to-do list at once.
Student: That makes sense. What about sleep — how important is it really?
Advisor: Critical. Sleep is when your brain consolidates what you've studied. Poor sleep doesn't just make you tired; it directly undermines learning.

lectureQuestions.explanation: The advisor explicitly advises the student to 'break tasks into smaller chunks rather than looking at your whole to-do list at once.'
wrongReasons: The advisor never mentions study location as a strategy.

While structure might be implied, the advisor's specific recommendation is about task chunking, not a strict daily schedule.

passage.title: Lecture: How Memory Consolidation Works During Sleep
passage.body: Professor: Most people think of sleep as simply a period of rest — the brain switching off for the night. But modern neuroscience tells us something far more interesting. Sleep is, in fact, one of the most cognitively active periods of your day. Let me explain what happens. When you fall asleep, you cycle through different stages roughly every 90 minutes. Early in the night, you spend more time in slow-wave sleep — also called deep sleep — and this is when the hippocampus replays the day's experiences and transfers information to the cortex for long-term storage. Later in the night, REM sleep becomes more dominant. REM is associated with emotional memory processing and creative connections between ideas. Think of it as the brain filing away what matters and making unexpected links. This is why people often say they 'slept on a problem' and woke up with a solution. If you cut your sleep short, you lose disproportionately more REM sleep, because REM occurs mostly in the second half of the night. So the idea that you can function well on five hours is, from a memory standpoint, quite mistaken.

lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor states that during slow-wave sleep 'the hippocampus replays the day's experiences and transfers information to the cortex for long-term storage.'

passage.title: Office Hours: Choosing a Research Methodology
passage.body:
Student: I wanted to ask for your advice on my research project. I'm trying to decide between qualitative and quantitative approaches and I'm not sure which fits my question better.
Professor: That's exactly the right question to start with. What is your research question?
Student: I want to understand why first-generation university students feel they don't belong on campus.
Professor: Right, so you're interested in lived experience — people's feelings, interpretations, and personal narratives. That points strongly toward qualitative methods. Interviews or focus groups would let you explore the nuances of how students construct their sense of belonging.
Student: But wouldn't a survey give me more representative data?
Professor: It might give you broader data — but breadth and depth are different things. A survey could tell you how many students feel this way, but not why, or what it actually feels like. Since your question asks 'why,' qualitative is the better fit.
Student: So the methodology should follow the research question, not the other way round?
Professor: Exactly. Never choose a method because it seems more rigorous or objective. Choose it because it answers your specific question. Quantitative methods are powerful, but they're not universally superior — it depends entirely on what you're trying to find out.

How many students experience feelings of not belonging

lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor says 'A survey could tell you how many students feel this way, but not why, or what it actually feels like,' identifying the count of affected students as what surveys provide.
wrongReasons: The professor explicitly says a survey cannot tell the student 'why' — that is the limitation he identifies.
The professor says a survey cannot tell what it 'actually feels like' — that too is listed as a limitation.

passage.title: Lecture: The History of the Internet
passage.body: Professor: The internet as we know it today did not appear overnight — it evolved over several decades through a combination of military investment, academic collaboration, and private innovation. Its origins lie in ARPANET, a project funded by the United States Department of Defense in the late 1960s. ARPANET was designed to allow computers at different universities and research institutions to share data, and crucially, it was built to be resilient — capable of rerouting data if part of the network was destroyed. In the 1970s and 1980s, the network expanded and the foundational communication protocols we still use today, TCP/IP, were developed and standardised. These protocols allowed different types of computers to communicate with each other seamlessly. The real turning point for public access came in 1991, when Tim Berners-Lee, a British scientist working at CERN, introduced the World Wide Web — a system of interlinked documents accessible through a browser. This transformed the internet from a tool for specialists into something ordinary people could use. Within a decade, the Web had changed commerce, communication, and culture on a global scale.

It was the first network to use TCP/IP communication protocols
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor states ARPANET 'was built to be resilient — capable of rerouting data if part of the network was destroyed,' identifying resilience as its key design feature.
wrongReasons: Encryption is never mentioned as a feature of ARPANET in the lecture.
ARPANET connected universities and research institutions to each other, not to government databases.

passage.title: Lecture: The History of the Internet
passage.body: Professor: The internet as we know it today did not appear overnight — it evolved over several decades through a combination of military investment, academic collaboration, and private innovation. Its origins lie in ARPANET, a project funded by the United States Department of Defense in the late 1960s. ARPANET was designed to allow computers at different universities and research institutions to share data, and crucially, it was built to be resilient — capable of rerouting data if part of the network was destroyed. In the 1970s and 1980s, the network expanded and the foundational communication protocols we still use today, TCP/IP, were developed and standardised. These protocols allowed different types of computers to communicate with each other seamlessly. The real turning point for public access came in 1991, when Tim Berners-Lee, a British scientist working at CERN, introduced the World Wide Web — a system of interlinked documents accessible through a browser. This transformed the internet from a tool for specialists into something ordinary people could use. Within a decade, the Web had changed commerce, communication, and culture on a global scale.

lectureQuestions.explanation: By tracing a line from the DoD-funded ARPANET to a global public network that 'changed commerce, communication, and culture,' the professor implies that military investment inadvertently produced a technology with enormous civilian impact — an unintended outcome of defence research.

passage.title: Campus Conversation: Group Project Disagreement
passage.body:
Priya: Marcus, I think we need to talk about how we're going to approach this project. I feel like we keep starting and stopping because we don't have a clear plan.
Marcus: I get that, but I think the best way for me to think is to just start drafting and see where things go. Rigid outlines kill my creativity.
Priya: I understand that works for you, but we're a team. If we all draft separately without agreeing on the structure first, we're going to end up with three completely different essays.
Marcus: Fair point. But I don't want to spend two weeks planning and then have no time to actually write.
Priya: I'm not suggesting two weeks of planning. Just an afternoon to agree on our key argument and how we'll divide the sections. We can still be flexible within that.
Marcus: Okay, I think I can work with that. A rough framework rather than a rigid outline.
Priya: Exactly. And if your section goes in a direction that changes the argument, we check in and adjust. Does that work?
Marcus: Yeah, that's fair. Let's schedule that meeting for tomorrow.

passage.title: Lecture: Why Coral Reefs Are Disappearing
passage.body: Professor: Coral reefs cover less than one percent of the ocean floor, yet they support roughly twenty-five percent of all marine species. They are one of the most biodiverse ecosystems on Earth — and they are disappearing at an alarming rate. The primary threat is ocean warming. When sea temperatures rise even one or two degrees Celsius above the normal summer maximum, corals expel the symbiotic algae that live in their tissues. These algae, called zooxanthellae, provide corals with up to ninety percent of their energy through photosynthesis. Without them, the coral turns white — this is bleaching. If temperatures stay elevated, the coral starves and dies. A second major threat is ocean acidification. As the ocean absorbs carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, it becomes more acidic. This reduces the availability of carbonate ions, which corals need to build their calcium carbonate skeletons. Essentially, the ocean is slowly dissolving the building blocks of reef structure. Together, these two stressors — warming and acidification — are compounding in ways that give reefs very little time to recover between disturbances. Scientists estimate that without significant emissions reductions, up to ninety percent of coral reefs could be severely degraded by 2050.

lectureQuestions.text: What role do zooxanthellae play in the health of coral?

lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor states that zooxanthellae 'provide corals with up to ninety percent of their energy through photosynthesis,' making energy supply their primary function.
wrongReasons: No mention of predator protection or toxic compounds appears in the lecture.
The professor distinguishes between zooxanthellae (energy suppliers) and calcium carbonate skeletons (structural elements) — they are different things.
Temperature regulation is not attributed to zooxanthellae; warming is identified as the threat that causes their expulsion.

passage.title: Tutorial: Understanding Statistical Significance
passage.body:
Student: I ran my analysis and it says p equals 0.03, but I'm not sure what that actually means.
Tutor: Good question. The p-value tells you the probability of getting a result at least as extreme as yours, assuming the null hypothesis is true.
Student: What's the null hypothesis again?
Tutor: It's the default assumption that there's no real effect or relationship — that any pattern in your data is due to chance. So if p is 0.03, it means there's only a 3% chance of seeing your result if the null hypothesis were true.
Student: So a lower p-value means a stronger result?
Tutor: Not exactly. It means the result is less likely to be explained by chance alone. But it doesn't tell you the size or importance of the effect. A result can be statistically significant and still be trivially small in practical terms.
Student: What's the usual threshold for significance?
Tutor: Most fields use 0.05. Below that, the result is conventionally considered statistically significant. But that threshold is somewhat arbitrary — it's a guideline, not a law of nature.
Student: So my 0.03 result is significant?
Tutor: By the conventional threshold, yes. But always report the exact p-value and describe the effect size too. Significance alone doesn't tell the whole story.

lectureQuestions.text: According to the tutor, what does a p-value of 0.03 specifically indicate?
choices: The research hypothesis has a 97% chance of being correct
There is a 3% probability of observing the result if the null hypothesis were true
The effect discovered is large and practically important
The study has a 3% margin of error in its measurements
lectureQuestions.explanation: The tutor says 'if p is 0.03, it means there's only a 3% chance of seeing your result if the null hypothesis were true,' which is the precise definition given.
wrongReasons: The tutor does not say the p-value gives the probability that the hypothesis is correct; this is a common misconception the tutor implicitly avoids.
The tutor explicitly warns that statistical significance 'doesn't tell you the size or importance of the effect,' so practical importance cannot be inferred from the p-value alone.
Margin of error is a different statistical concept related to confidence intervals, not p-values — it is not mentioned.

lectureQuestions.text: What is this lecture primarily about?
choices: The political revolutions that occurred alongside industrialisation in Britain
The factors that caused the Industrial Revolution to begin
How the Industrial Revolution spread from Britain to other countries
The social consequences of factory work for the working class
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor focuses on explaining the causes of the Industrial Revolution — coal, iron, labour supply, and the cotton industry — making causation the central concern of the lecture.
wrongReasons: Political revolutions are not discussed; the lecture focuses on economic and material causes.
The spread of industrialisation to other countries is not addressed; the lecture stays on Britain's conditions.
Social consequences for workers are not the focus; the lecture examines why the Revolution began, not its human cost.

passage.title: Lecture: The Causes of the Industrial Revolution
passage.body: Professor: The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain around 1760, was not a sudden event but the outcome of several interlocking conditions that came together at a particular time and place. Let's consider the key factors. First, Britain had abundant reserves of coal and iron ore, often located close to each other and near navigable waterways. This made it relatively cheap and practical to mine, smelt, and transport raw materials. Second, a series of agricultural improvements in the eighteenth century had displaced large numbers of rural workers, creating a mobile labour force willing to work in the new factories. This is sometimes called the enclosure movement. Third, Britain had a developed banking system, legal protections for patents and investments, and a colonial empire that provided both raw materials and markets. The cotton textile industry was the first to mechanise on a large scale — factories powered by water and later steam replaced cottage industries almost overnight. And underpinning all of this was steam power itself, which, once Watt improved the engine in the 1760s and 1770s, made it possible to locate factories away from rivers and to power machines at a scale previously unimaginable.

lectureQuestions.text: What role did the enclosure movement play in the Industrial Revolution, according to the professor?
choices: It provided landowners with capital to invest in new factories
It forced rural workers off the land, creating a labour force for the factories
It allowed the consolidation of coal and iron mining operations
It encouraged the development of the cotton textile industry in rural areas
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor says agricultural improvements 'displaced large numbers of rural workers, creating a mobile labour force willing to work in the new factories,' identifying this population shift as the enclosure movement's industrial contribution.
wrongReasons: Landowner capital from enclosure is not mentioned; the connection drawn is between displaced workers and the factory labour supply.
Coal and iron mining consolidation is not attributed to enclosure; resource proximity is discussed separately as a geographic advantage.
The cotton industry mechanised in factory settings, not rural areas — enclosure created the urban workforce, not a rural textile expansion.

passage.title: Lecture: The Causes of the Industrial Revolution
passage.body: Professor: The Industrial Revolution, which began in Britain around 1760, was not a sudden event but the outcome of several interlocking conditions that came together at a particular time and place. Let's consider the key factors. First, Britain had abundant reserves of coal and iron ore, often located close to each other and near navigable waterways. This made it relatively cheap and practical to mine, smelt, and transport raw materials. Second, a series of agricultural improvements in the eighteenth century had displaced large numbers of rural workers, creating a mobile labour force willing to work in the new factories. This is sometimes called the enclosure movement. Third, Britain had a developed banking system, legal protections for patents and investments, and a colonial empire that provided both raw materials and markets. The cotton textile industry was the first to mechanise on a large scale — factories powered by water and later steam replaced cottage industries almost overnight. And underpinning all of this was steam power itself, which, once Watt improved the engine in the 1760s and 1770s, made it possible to locate factories away from rivers and to power machines at a scale previously unimaginable.

lectureQuestions.text: What does the professor mean when he describes the Industrial Revolution as the outcome of 'several interlocking conditions'?
choices: The Revolution was caused by one primary factor that triggered all other changes
Each contributing factor depended on and reinforced the others, making it impossible to identify a single cause
The conditions in Britain were largely copied from industrial developments in other European nations
Industrial growth required the simultaneous discovery of new scientific principles
lectureQuestions.explanation: 'Interlocking' implies mutual dependence — coal required labour to mine it, labour needed the enclosure movement to displace it, factories needed banking systems to finance them, and steam power needed coal to run. The professor's structure implies no single cause alone would have been sufficient.
wrongReasons: The professor explicitly says it was not 'a sudden event' caused by one thing; he lists multiple interlocking factors.
Britain's conditions are presented as unique — the professor does not suggest they were copied from other nations.
Scientific discovery is not presented as a precondition; the practical improvements (like Watt's engine) were technological adaptations, not new scientific principles.

lectureQuestions.text: What problem does the student bring to the academic advisor?
choices: She is struggling to pass her exams despite working hard
She is finding it difficult to manage both part-time work and her studies
She wants to take on more work hours to pay for tuition fees
She is considering dropping out of university for financial reasons
lectureQuestions.explanation: The student says 'I've been working about twenty hours a week at a cafe and my grades are starting to slip,' clearly identifying the work-study balance as her core problem.
wrongReasons: Poor exam performance is a consequence of her situation, but the underlying problem she brings is the work-study conflict, not study technique.

passage.title: Campus Conversation: Balancing Work and Studies
passage.body:
Student: I've been working about twenty hours a week at a cafe and my grades are starting to slip. I don't know how to manage everything.
Advisor: That's a heavy load. Is the work financially necessary, or is there any flexibility there?
Student: It's necessary. I'm covering my rent and living costs.
Advisor: Okay, so reducing hours significantly isn't really an option. In that case, let's think about how to use your study time more efficiently. Have you tried time-blocking — scheduling specific hours for specific tasks?
Student: Not really. I just study when I can.
Advisor: That approach tends to leave too much to chance. If you map out your week, including your shifts, you can see exactly how many study hours you actually have and protect them.
Student: I suppose I never really thought of it that way.
Advisor: Also, talk to your lecturers. Most are very understanding if a student is transparent about working and just needs a bit of guidance on prioritising. You don't need to struggle in silence.
Student: I didn't realise it was okay to do that.
Advisor: Absolutely. And look into whether you qualify for a hardship fund or bursary — it might reduce the number of hours you need to work.

lectureQuestions.text: What specific time management strategy does the advisor recommend to the student?
choices: Studying in short bursts of twenty minutes with frequent breaks
Mapping out the week and scheduling specific study hours around work shifts
Joining a study group to make revision more efficient
Focusing only on the most important assignments and ignoring smaller tasks
lectureQuestions.explanation: The advisor recommends 'time-blocking — scheduling specific hours for specific tasks' and mapping out the week to see and protect available study hours.
wrongReasons: Short study bursts and breaks are not discussed; the advisor's recommendation is about weekly planning and protecting study time.
Study groups are not mentioned; the strategy is individual time-mapping.
Prioritising certain assignments over others is not suggested; the advisor's strategy is about using all available time more deliberately.

lectureQuestions.text: What is the main distinction the professor draws in this lecture?
choices: Between vaccines and natural immunity
Between the innate and adaptive immune systems
Between bacterial infections and viral infections
Between the roles of T cells and B cells exclusively
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor structures the entire lecture around the difference between innate immunity (fast, non-specific) and adaptive immunity (slower, targeted, with memory), making this the central distinction.
wrongReasons: Vaccines are mentioned briefly as an example of memory cells but are not the main distinction the lecture is built around.
Bacterial vs viral infections are not discussed; the lecture focuses on immune system components.
While T cells and B cells are mentioned as part of adaptive immunity, they are details within the broader innate-versus-adaptive framework.

passage.title: Lecture: How the Human Immune System Works
passage.body: Professor: The human immune system is one of the most sophisticated defence networks in biology. To understand it, we need to distinguish between two broad components: the innate immune system and the adaptive immune system. The innate system is your first line of defence. It responds rapidly — within minutes to hours — to any foreign substance, using general mechanisms that don't distinguish between specific threats. Inflammation, fever, and the activity of natural killer cells are all part of this response. It's fast but non-specific. The adaptive immune system, by contrast, is slower to activate — it can take several days — but it is exquisitely targeted. When immune cells called lymphocytes encounter a specific pathogen, they produce antibodies tailored to that threat. Crucially, some of these lymphocytes become memory cells, which persist in the body long after the infection is cleared. If the same pathogen appears again, the response is dramatically faster and stronger. This is the biological basis of immunity — and of vaccination, which trains the adaptive system without requiring you to suffer the actual disease. Together, these two systems create a layered defence that is both rapid and precise.

lectureQuestions.text: According to the professor, what is a key characteristic of the innate immune system?
choices: It produces antibodies specific to each type of pathogen
It responds rapidly but without targeting specific threats
It creates memory cells that recognise previous infections
It takes several days to activate after initial exposure
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor describes the innate system as 'fast but non-specific,' responding 'within minutes to hours' using 'general mechanisms that don't distinguish between specific threats.'
wrongReasons: Specific antibody production is a feature of the adaptive immune system, not the innate system.
Memory cells are described as a feature of the adaptive immune system; the innate system does not create them.
The several-day activation period describes the adaptive immune system; the innate system responds in minutes to hours.

lectureQuestions.text: What is the purpose of this office hours meeting?
choices: The professor is returning a graded essay to the student
The student is receiving feedback on the introduction she has written
The student wants help choosing a thesis topic
The professor is explaining how to use secondary sources in research
lectureQuestions.explanation: The student brings her introduction for feedback and the professor provides detailed comments on how to improve it — making feedback on the written introduction the clear purpose of the meeting.
wrongReasons: The essay is not graded; the professor is providing formative feedback on a draft introduction.
A topic is not being chosen; the student already has a thesis on social media and adolescent identity.
Secondary sources are briefly mentioned but the meeting's focus is on the introduction's structure, not source use in general.

passage.title: Office Hours: Writing a Strong Introduction
passage.body:
Student: I've brought my introduction for you to look at. I'm not sure it's quite right but I don't know what's missing.
Professor: Let me read through it. Okay — your opening is fine, you set the context well. But I'm not seeing a clear argument. You describe the topic — social media and adolescent identity — but you never tell me what you are actually claiming about it.
Student: I thought the argument came in the body of the essay.
Professor: Your evidence and analysis do, yes. But your introduction needs to signal the argument from the start. Think of it as a promise to your reader: here is what I will argue and why it matters.
Student: So I need a thesis statement?
Professor: Exactly. One or two sentences that state your specific claim — not just your topic. And ideally a sentence or two explaining what the reader will gain from your argument.
Student: What about the sources — I have a lot of background literature in there.
Professor: That's a balance to strike. Some context from the literature is useful, but an introduction shouldn't become a mini literature review. Save the detailed engagement with sources for the body.
Student: That makes sense. So: hook, context, thesis, significance — and keep it tight?
Professor: Exactly. You've just described the ideal introduction structure.

lectureQuestions.text: What does the professor identify as the main weakness in the student's introduction?
choices: The opening sentences do not establish the topic clearly
There are too many quotations from secondary sources
The introduction does not contain a clear argument or thesis statement
The writing style is too informal for an academic essay
lectureQuestions.explanation: The professor says 'I'm not seeing a clear argument. You describe the topic...but you never tell me what you are actually claiming about it,' identifying the missing thesis as the central problem.
wrongReasons: The professor says 'your opening is fine, you set the context well' — the opening sentences are praised, not criticised.
The professor notes there is a lot of background literature but frames this as a secondary issue to balance, not the main weakness.
Writing style is never mentioned; the feedback focuses entirely on structure and argumentative content.
